title: Desperation - The Ran(King) of King - Part 3
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kpcxQ1XCrq8

Daniel (@dungeondive) reaches 1996's Desperation in The Ran(King) of King — one of King's big capital-G-Good versus capital-E-Evil epics, and a book he adores. In the desert ghost town of Desperation, Nevada, miners long ago dug too deep and freed Tak, an ancient evil (described as more disease than god) that controls beasts and possesses the dead. Now wearing the body of the local sheriff, Tak arrests passing travellers and locks them in cells; they must band together — led by a boy, David, a conduit for a mysterious, often-cruel God — to escape and destroy it.

This is his second read, and he loved it more. He calls it a firecracker: where King usually paces slowly, Desperation hits the ground running and rarely lets up across ~600 pages — violent, dark, fun, exactly what most people picture as "a Stephen King novel." His one persistent gripe with modern King surfaces (younger characters who don't talk like real young people — Cynthia sounds like a '70s stoner), but the storytelling mastery carries it. He's fascinated by David's wrestling with a cruel God, and flags heavy Dark Tower DNA: an ka-tet of heroes, the can toi, invented Mid-World language.

It seizes first place on the board.

----

Does Desperation's relentless, punch-you-in-the-face pace make it prime King for you — and how do you feel about its heavy religious core?
